title: Smoked Eggplant Alboronía with Fonio and Grains of Paradise
tagline: An Andalusi vegetable stew that learned its grain and its pepper from Timbuktu.
scenario: In 1324 Mansa Musa crossed the Sahara on his hajj and spent so much gold in Cairo that Egyptian chroniclers tracked the depression for a decade. In our timeline the embassy went home and the gold road kept its old terminus. In this one, the returning caravan carried envoys past Sijilmasa and on to Nasrid Granada, where a nervous emirate squeezed between Castile and Fez bought itself a patron. The pact of 1326 traded Malian gold and salt for Granadan silk, paper, and safe harbor. Within a generation the caravans carried kitchen goods: sacks of fonio, which steams faster than any wheat, and grains of paradise, the peppery cardamom cousin that cost a fraction of Indian pepper routed through Venice. Granada's cooks folded both into alboronía, the eggplant stew already three centuries old. Our alboronía had to wait for American tomatoes and peppers. This one never needed them. The stew stayed dark, sweet with honey and vinegar, hot with melegueta, and it was served over a grain that drinks sauce like it was born for it.

items.item: olive oil, plus more to finish
saffron threads, steeped in 3 tbsp hot water

method: Char the eggplant: set the eggplants whole over a gas flame or under a hot broiler, turning, 10-12 minutes, until the skins are blackened and the flesh has collapsed. Cool, peel, and tear the flesh into wide ribbons. Do not rinse; a few specks of char are the point.

Build the sofrito: warm the olive oil in a wide pot over medium heat and cook the onion with a pinch of salt for 8 minutes until soft and golden. Add the garlic, cumin, and cinnamon and cook 1 minute more, until the spices smell toasted.
Simmer the stew: add the zucchini, chickpeas, saffron with its water, broth, and the salt. Simmer uncovered for 12 minutes, until the zucchini is tender and the liquid has begun to tighten.

Fold in the eggplant: add the eggplant ribbons, honey, and vinegar and simmer gently 8-10 minutes more, until the stew is thick, glossy, and jammy rather than brothy. Taste for salt and sharpness; it should land just on the sweet side of agrodolce.
Steam the fonio: bring the water to a boil with a pinch of salt, stir in the fonio, cover, and pull off the heat. Rest 5 minutes, then fluff with a fork; the grains should be dry and separate.
Crush the grains of paradise: pound them coarsely in a mortar. Stir half into the stew off the heat; their heat fades fast when boiled.
Serve: pile the fonio into shallow bowls and spoon the stew over. Scatter the remaining melegueta, the almonds, and the mint, and finish with a thread of olive oil.

queries: "grains of paradise" eggplant chickpea stew recipe
fonio eggplant recipe West African grain
fonio stew Moorish OR Andalusian OR Spanish recipe
verification.verdict: Alboronía is widely published as an Andalusian pisto made with tomato and pepper; no version uses fonio or grains of paradise, and no published recipe serves any eggplant-chickpea stew over fonio. Nearest neighbors are Lebanese and Moroccan eggplant-chickpea stews and plain fonio pilafs, none seasoned with melegueta.
